homepage.databus_definition: The open platform for real data on the quality of public transportation lines in Israel
homepage.website_goal: The purpose of the site is to improve the quality of public transportation in Israel by providing reliable information to journalists, citizens, transportation companies, and government officials in Israel.

dashboard_page_title: Public transport operators according to planned trips
dashboard_page_description: A graphic display of public transportation rides - ratio of successful actual rides / total rides scheduled. Grouped by bus operators (Egged, Dan, Metropolin etc)
no_data_from_ETL: We’re having trouble retrieving data from the server, We're working to resolve this issue
timeline_page_description: Displays actual vs scheduled stop times by user-given bus operator, route number & stop name
time_based_map_page_description: Displays all buses locations on map based on user-given date, time & minutes range
gaps_page_description: Displays routes that did not take place according to schedule by user-given bus operator, route number. route & date
line_heatmap_page_description: Shows which parts of the selected route were reached by each bus during the selected service day
gaps_patterns_page_description: A graphic display of routes performed according to schedule, grouped by time of day\severity, by user-given bus operator, route number, route & date
singleline_map_page_description: Display of bus route on map by user-given bus operator, vehicle number, route number, route, date & time
open_menu_description: Displays different options and parameters which are accessible in the application
timestamp_list: A list of all stop times at the selected stop
hits_not_found: No planned or actual trips found
dashboard_tooltip_content:
A GPS is attached to every line in Israel that reports the location of the bus every few moments.
So what is a missed trip? This is a trip that was planned, but was not reported to have taken place in the GPS data. You can see it in the app for example, but when you wait at the station, it will never arrive
dashboard_page_graph_title: Exit percentages of total trips by time
watch_locations_in_range: View bus locations within a range of
loading_times_tooltip_content: Loading bus times. Current times available to use
how_to_donate_text: Behind the scenes of the projects is a small, dedicated team that takes care of organizing development and hackathon meetings and server maintenance, which has its feet between government offices to persuade, press, and swing to release more and more repositories; Make sure the media knows our work and also knows how to contact us for data and analytics, and does a lot of ongoing work to make our projects change in the world. We need community support to continue to work for a more open government. There were knights of transparency and joined in monthly support.
donation_through_bank_reccomendation: It is recommended to donate a bank transfer to the public knowledge workshop bank account, as this fee is lower
donation_through_bank_details_additional: Please specify a full name and contact info@hasadna.org.il after the donation so we can send a receipt
what_is_website_paragraph: The database website of the workshop for public knowledge presents data on the quality of public transportation lines in Israel (reliability, accuracy, route). Databus actually connects several government information sources:
planning_information: The planning information - GTFS - which is published every day and contains the details of the lines, station locations, transit times, etc.
performance_information: Performance information - SIRI - Every bus in Israel is attached to a GPS transmitter that reports the location of the bus every few seconds.
discovered_mistake: We discovered an error or outdated information, what do we do?
discovered_mistake_paragraph: Since all the information presented on the site is based on the official sources of information, it is recommended to contact the publisher for clarification or correction of the problem. The "Databus" team will be happy to help you understand who the government entity is that published the data or information that requires clarification or correction.
and_smaller_donors: And other small contributions from my friends and fans of the workshop.
bug_date_alert: The end date can not be earlier than the start date
bug_date_invalid_format: Please enter a date in the correct format
funding_paragraph: The Databus project is developed by the Hasadna for public knowledge, by the contributions of volunteers, and based upon

complaints.cleanliness: Cleanliness and Condition of the Bus
complaints.event_hour_between_wait: Event time must be between wait times
complaints.first_declaration: I hereby confirm that I will be ready to testify at the Ministry of Transportation
complaints.second_declaration: I hereby confirm that I will be ready to testify in court
complaints.complaint_details_required: In order for us to check your request, please specify in the request which modes of transportation you wish to match, line numbers, operator names, station SKU, and relevant times.

lineProfile.title: Profile for Line
lineProfile.notFound: We couldn't find the line you were looking for :(

errorPage.title: Oops, something went wrong
errorPage.text2: If the issue persists, please contact us at:

aboutPage.title: About us
aboutPage.privacyText: <0>Google Analytics</0> service is embedded in the website to analyze the usage patterns and positioning of the website in search engines. This code reveals to the operators of the service information regarding the behavior of the users. <1>Learn more</1> about the privacy policy of the service.

licenseInfo.text: All information displayed on the website is based on data published by government sources. Usage of the information is subject to the <0>{{licenseName}}</0> license of <1>Creative Commons</1>

contactLinksText.donations: Servers cost money - help us continue to maintain and develop the project!

aboutPage.contributorsText: Databus is developed by the Public Knowledge Workshop, by the contributions of volunteers, and based upon
aboutPage.contributorsReadMore: <0>Read more</0> about how you can contribute to the project

dashboard_page_graph_title_hour: Percentage of rides out, by hour
dashboard_page_graph_title_day: Percentage of rides out, by day

reportBug.description: This form is designed so that we can receive feedback and improve the application. Please be aware that the submitted information will be available to the public.
reportBug.error: An error occurred while sending the report. Please try again later.
reportBug.success: The report was sent successfully. Thank you for your help!

tasks.title: What affect does the traffic have on the overall execusion time of bus ride?
tasks.description: On 'Databus' API you can find the exact locations of every bus in Israel in the past few years. What roads have traffic, what roads have significant gap between rush hours and other times of the day? By data analysis we believe we can point out specific roads where the traffic jams mostly are. We'd love your help!

tasks.title: Calculate time estimation for when the bus left it's origin and arrived different stations
tasks.description: How will we know whether the bus left on time, early or late? Usually, the devices on the busses are activated a few minutes before the begining of the ride, therefore we have many location samples of busses before their departure. Are there any busses departing from their origin station before the right time? If so - it's wrong, and we'll love your help estimating and monitoring it.

tasks.title: How to classify bus-lines to meaningful geographical clusters?
tasks.description: We Would love to have statistics by geographic region on our site. Do Jerusalem lines perform better than Beer Sheva lines? For that we need to understand and decide how to approach Jerusalem-Beer Sheva line, for example. Does it belong to both regions? Maybe we should exclude such lines? We're open for your thoughts and ideas, and you're welcome to offer different aggregations and help integrate them into the system.

tasks.title: Bus Bunching
tasks.description: Wikipedia - In public transport, bus bunching is a phenomenon whereby two or more transit vehicles that were scheduled at regular intervals along a common route instead bunch together and form a platoon. This occurs when leading vehicles are unable to keep their schedule and fall behind to such an extent that trailing vehicles catch up to them.

hackathonPage.registrationClosedNote: Thanks to everyone who signed up! To join the waitlist, ping us on WhatsApp.
hackathonPage.openFormInNewTab: Open it in a new tab →

paths.title: Choose your path
paths.intro: Four tracks, each with a different audience and difficulty range. Pick a track now and find the specific challenges further down the page.

hackathonPage.nextSteps: If you continue after the event
hackathonPage.noChallengesForFilter: No challenges match this filter — try a different tier

trackDescription.harness: Build tools that make AI assistants genuinely useful in a dev workflow — scripts, hooks, and skills that turn Claude into a real team member
trackDescription.ai-readiness: Make Databus's data readable by AI agents — llms.txt, a Markdown-returning endpoint, an MCP server. If you write code with AI help, you'll benefit from the output
trackDescription.mapping: The project has features and PRs that have been waiting for attention — discover what exists, what's broken, and what can be revived. You can ship a real PR with no prior knowledge
trackDescription.data: Analyze and improve Israeli public transit data. Best fit for government officials, bus operators, and NPOs — your domain knowledge is the most valuable input here

hackathonPage.openContributions: These challenges are starting ideas, not a closed list. Have your own idea? Share it with us at the start of the evening.

hero.title: Databus Hackathon · June 9, 2026
hero.subtitle: One evening, challenges for every level, open source for Israeli public transit.
hero.audience: Developers, designers, Ministry of Transport, bus operators, NPOs, domain experts — all welcome
hero.location: Applitools, One Towers, Ramat Gan (10 min from Savidor train station, 2 min from Aba Hilel light rail)

personas.title: Who is this for?
personas.intro: We're not just a coders' hackathon. Databus's power comes from mixing developers, domain experts, policy people, and NPOs. Everyone joins mixed teams — domain experts mostly embed in Data & Algorithms tracks, but every track welcomes you.

developer.name: Developer
developer.invite: Sharpen your AI skills while contributing to real open source.

government.name: Ministry of Transport / regulator
government.invite: Join a dev team as a domain expert — point the code at real field problems.

operator.name: Bus operator
operator.invite: Be the voice of the driver and dispatcher inside a dev team — Data & Algorithms especially.

npo.name: NPOs & domain experts
npo.invite: Supply context and user experience to teams building features that serve the public.

whyCome.title: What you'll get out of it

contribute.title: Contribute to Israeli open source
contribute.body: Databus serves journalists, citizens, regulators — every feature you ship reaches the real world.

learn.title: Work with experts
learn.body: Meet developers, domain experts, and policy people — and work together on a real problem.

sunkCost.title: Walk out with something shipped
sunkCost.body: An open PR, a requirements doc, or a prototype. Those who want to continue can join the weekly Monday volunteering at Bet HaAmutot, Tel Aviv.

challenges.title: Challenges
challenges.intro: Every challenge is sized so you can ship an MVP in 90 minutes. These are starting ideas — not a closed list. Have your own idea? Share it with us at the start of the evening. Filter by tier, pick a track, expand a card to read the full brief.

harness-tests-ci.title: Write tests and open the agent feedback loop
harness-tests-ci.oneLiner: Without tests, the agent doesn't know if it broke something — and neither do you.
harness-tests-ci.ship: A PR with unit tests for one currently-untested module + a GitHub Actions job that runs them on every PR.
harness-tests-ci.learn: Jest, GitHub Actions, writing tests whose output is legible to an AI agent.
harness-tests-ci.brief: When an AI agent edits code, the fastest way to know it didn't break anything is tests running in CI and returning clear output. The frontend has several modules with zero test coverage: gap detection logic, time-calculation utilities, the Countdown component, and more. Pick one module, write unit tests, and add (or improve) the GitHub Actions job that runs them. Requirement: the agent will need to run the tests and interpret the output — make sure it's readable.
done: At least 3 unit tests passing on current code
At least 1 test covering an edge case
GitHub Actions job running Jest on every PR
harness-tests-ci.nextSteps: Expand to integration tests, add a coverage report, wire into a Claude skill that runs tests and summarizes failures.

harness-backend-devenv.title: Agent-ready backend environment — all repos, one command
harness-backend-devenv.oneLiner: The backend spans 7 repos. Build a single entry point an agent can actually use.
harness-backend-devenv.ship: A devcontainer.json + one Claude skill that runs a real query against the Stride API and returns results.
harness-backend-devenv.learn: Databus backend architecture, devcontainers, Claude skills with Python.
harness-backend-devenv.brief: The Databus backend is split across open-bus-pipelines (orchestration), stride-db (schema), stride-etl (processing), siri-etl (real-time), and more. A new contributor — let alone an AI agent — needs to know what to clone, what to start, and in what order. Build a devcontainer that ships with stride-client pre-installed, a .claude/settings.json with approved Python commands, and one Claude skill that runs a query against the live Stride API and returns readable results.
done: devcontainer.json works — `devcontainer open` lands you in Python + stride-client
stride-client connects to Stride API and returns real data
One Claude skill runs a query and returns a human-readable answer
harness-backend-devenv.nextSteps: Add more skills, wire to a local PostgreSQL, publish as the recommended entry point for new contributors across all Hasadna repos.

harness-skill-backend-tests.title: Claude skill for backend test authoring and running
harness-skill-backend-tests.oneLiner: Some repos have tests; others have none. Build a skill that handles both.
harness-skill-backend-tests.ship: A Claude skill that guides an agent to discover, run, and write pytest tests in at least one backend repo.
harness-skill-backend-tests.learn: How to write a skill description that navigates an agent through ambiguity; pytest patterns in Python codebases.
harness-skill-backend-tests.brief: The Databus backend spans repos like siri-etl, stride-etl, and open-bus-stride-client — Python codebases with wildly different test coverage. Some have pytest suites already; some have zero tests at all. Build a Claude skill that handles both cases: it helps the agent discover what exists, run it, and write new tests for uncovered modules. The real challenge isn't the first working version — it's the iteration. Run the skill, watch where the agent goes wrong, rewrite the description, run again. A well-written skill gets the agent 80% of the way without a human hand-holding.
done: Skill works on a backend repo that already has tests
Skill successfully guides the agent to write at least one new test in a repo with zero coverage
You ran it, saw it fail at something, improved it — at least one full iteration
harness-skill-backend-tests.nextSteps: Generalize across all Hasadna backend repos; publish as a shared skill library new contributors can install in one command.

harness-hooks-gate.title: Hooks that stop AI from pushing broken code
harness-hooks-gate.oneLiner: Automatic guardrails for code written by an agent.
harness-hooks-gate.ship: A set of hooks in `.claude/hooks/` that runs lint+tests before every commit and sends the agent back to fix.
harness-hooks-gate.brief: When an AI agent writes code, you need fences. We'll design a hook setup that catches problems at the right moment — not just in CI.
done: Hooks at `pre-commit` and `stop`
The AI receives the output and knows how to fix
Live demo on a test task
harness-hooks-gate.nextSteps: Add more gates (security scan, type check) and publish as a template for other projects.

ai-llms-txt.title: Add llms.txt to Databus
ai-llms-txt.oneLiner: First step toward making the site readable by LLMs.
ai-llms-txt.ship: A PR that adds `llms.txt` at the site root with the 8 most important pages.
ai-llms-txt.learn: The llms.txt standard, how AI agents load context from sites.
ai-llms-txt.brief: llms.txt is the standard way to give LLMs a map of your site. We'll fill in a template, point at the critical pages, and open a PR.
done: Spec-compliant llms.txt file
Points to 8 main pages
Available in Hebrew and English
ai-llms-txt.nextSteps: Add `llms-full.txt` with full page content, and verify Claude/ChatGPT understand the data.

ai-markdown-export.title: Markdown export for the line profile page
ai-markdown-export.oneLiner: One URL, clean output, agents are happy.
ai-markdown-export.ship: An endpoint that returns the same content as the line profile page in Markdown format.
ai-markdown-export.learn: How to structure content for AI agents (not just humans).
ai-markdown-export.brief: The line profile page is hard for an agent to read. We'll add a path like `/profile/:id.md` that returns clean Markdown — no JavaScript, no CSS.
done: URL works for 3 sample lines
Content matches the regular page
Claude/ChatGPT can read it and answer questions
ai-markdown-export.nextSteps: Extend to other pages (gaps, map) and wire into llms.txt.

ai-mcp-prototype.title: First MCP server for Databus
ai-mcp-prototype.ship: A basic MCP server exposing one query (e.g. "find skipped trips for line 17א") with an end-to-end demo.
ai-mcp-prototype.learn: The MCP protocol, how to wire LLMs to live data.
ai-mcp-prototype.brief: MCP is the protocol that connects LLMs to external tools. We'll build a minimal MCP server wrapping one query from the Stride API and demo a real question.
done: Server runs locally
Claude Desktop / Cursor connects
One query returns a real answer
ai-mcp-prototype.nextSteps: Add more endpoints, publish as npm/pypi package, list on the public MCP registry.

mapping-stride-audit.title: Explore `open-bus-stride-client` and map what's usable
mapping-stride-audit.oneLiner: A Python library that wraps the Stride API — what can it do, what's missing?
mapping-stride-audit.ship: A PR with a README section (or separate doc) that maps the library's capabilities against current frontend needs.
mapping-stride-audit.learn: How the Stride REST API works, pandas + transit data, evaluating a Python library as a frontend consumer.
mapping-stride-audit.brief: `open-bus-stride-client` is an actively maintained Python client that wraps the Stride REST API. It can load routes into pandas DataFrames, retrieve arrivals, SIRI vehicle locations, GTFS schedules, and run accessibility analysis. The repo also has Jupyter notebooks with real worked examples — a great starting point. The frontend currently calls the API directly — but does the Python client expose things the frontend can't easily reach? There's also a Figma file with visualization designs that were never built; one of them might connect directly to stride-client data.
done: Ran at least 3 different queries using the library
Document of capabilities vs. what the frontend already shows
At least one concrete suggestion for a new feature enabled by the library
mapping-stride-audit.nextSteps: Implement one of the suggested features — either as a new backend endpoint or as an MCP tool.

mapping-resurrect-pr.title: Resurrect a stale PR
mapping-resurrect-pr.oneLiner: Several contributors left work nearly done. Finish it.
mapping-resurrect-pr.ship: An old PR merged to main.
mapping-resurrect-pr.learn: Git history management, merging old changes, communicating with the maintainer.
mapping-resurrect-pr.brief: Some PRs were opened but never crossed the finish line — usually due to conflicts or lack of time. Pick one from a curated shortlist, resolve conflicts, push to merge.
done: PR passes review
Tests pass
PR merged to main
mapping-resurrect-pr.nextSteps: Keep closing more PRs — create a priority list for the maintainer.

data-new-chart.title: New visualization for transit data
data-new-chart.oneLiner: What insights are you missing? Add them to an existing or new page.
data-new-chart.ship: A new chart, map, or table — in an existing page (gaps, operator, line profile) or a dedicated new page.
data-new-chart.learn: Recharts, data shaping, picking the right visualization. Domain experts: how to choose the metric that matters most to regulators.
data-new-chart.brief: There's a lot of transit data that hasn't been visualized yet. The existing dashboard is unreliable and hidden from the menu — so pick an existing page like the gaps page, operator page, or line profile, and add a visualization that shows a new metric. Or open a new dedicated page if the metric doesn't fit any existing one. Decide at the start which page you're targeting.
done: Visualization embedded in an existing or new page
Shows real data
Clear title and description
data-new-chart.nextSteps: Add filtering by date, operator, and geographic region.

data-arrival-gap-fix.title: Fix ride-gap detection edge cases
data-arrival-gap-fix.oneLiner: When a ride starts late or two rides are adjacent, the algorithm gets confused.
data-arrival-gap-fix.ship: A PR with the algorithmic fix + unit tests that fail before and pass after.
data-arrival-gap-fix.learn: Matching algorithm between GTFS (planned) and SIRI (actual), transit data edge cases. Domain experts are critical — they'll tell you what the 'right answer' is.
data-arrival-gap-fix.brief: The gaps page tracks entire rides (trips) that didn't happen — not individual stops. Each ride has a plannedStartTime from GTFS and an actualStartTime from SIRI. The problem: when a ride departs late, or when two adjacent rides run on the same line, the algorithm may mark one as 'missing' and one as 'extra' instead of matching them. An operator or regulator can help define the acceptable time threshold before a ride counts as 'missing'.
done: Unit test that fails before the fix, passes after
Code review with maintainer
Documentation of the time threshold chosen and why
data-arrival-gap-fix.nextSteps: Cover more edge cases — route detours, emergency trips, duplicates.

data-bus-bunching.title: Bus bunching detection
data-bus-bunching.oneLiner: When two buses arrive together — a known issue, let's address it.
data-bus-bunching.ship: A prototype algorithm that detects bunching from SIRI data.
data-bus-bunching.learn: Real-time data algorithms, operational definition of bunching with a domain expert.
data-bus-bunching.brief: Bus bunching hurts service reliability. We'll define what bunching means in numbers with an operator expert, run detection on historical data, present findings in the demo.
done: Algorithm runs on one day of real data
Results displayed in a chart or table
Domain expert confirms detection makes sense
data-bus-bunching.nextSteps: Wire into the dashboard, add alerts, publish a regulator-facing report.

nonDev.title: How do non-devs participate?
nonDev.body: On hackathon day, during the half-hour mingling, mentors will match you to a dev team working on a challenge that fits your domain expertise. Default: Data & Algorithms, where your knowledge is most critical. Your contribution will be part of the team's demo and you'll get credit on the PR.

mentors.title: Mentors
mentors.body: Each working group will have a mentor to help you navigate the codebase and the project. Find them by their name tag.

sponsors.title: Our Sponsors
sponsors.applitools: Applitools is sponsoring this hackathon — hosting the venue and providing us with a free open-source licence to Applitools Eyes. That licence helps us ship visual regression tests faster and with better coverage across the whole project.
sponsors.callout: Want to sponsor a future event? We're looking for companies that want to host a hackathon or make a long-term contribution to open-source public transit software in Israel.

registration.title: Registration
registration.body: Filling in the form below is mandatory to participate. Submitting the form is not enough — your registration is only confirmed once you receive an approval email from us. We confirm registrations manually so we can assign you to the right team. Registration closes June 3 at midnight.

bring.a: Developers: a code-ready laptop with git. Everyone else: just yourself and your knowledge.

experience.a: No! There are Starter challenges, and domain experts embed in dev teams.

free.a: Yes. Light refreshments will be served.

regulator.q: I'm from a regulator / operator — what's my role?
regulator.a: You'll be matched to a dev team, by default in Data & Algorithms, and serve as the domain expert who points the team at real problems.

devenv.q: I don't have a local dev environment set up — can I still code?
devenv.a: Yes. Open the repo in Gitpod for a browser-based VS Code environment with everything pre-installed: https://gitpod.io/#https://github.com/hasadna/open-bus-map-search
